text:
MEMPHIS, Tenn. (WMC) - There’s a “throw down” happening at the Paris Olympics, and it’s a first, as breakdancing makes its debut at the 2024 games.

Whether they’re “power heads” or “footwork cats,” b-boys and b-girls from around the world answered the “call out” to show their moves on a global stage.

We met up with a couple of Memphis breakers to learn how those “super crews” might put together their “sets” at the Olympics.

Brandon Marshall, known as “B-boy Nosey” in his Illville Crew, has been breaking for about 23 years.

”But when I was 14-15 years old and I got into high school, it was a way for me to impress girls and just impress my classmates and things,” he explained about his start with the dance genre.

We caught up with “B-boy Nosey” and “B-girl Nana” Tsujikawa at New Ballet Ensemble and School of Dance in Midtown Memphis, founded by Katie Smythe in 2002 “to address the lack of access to excellence in dance.”

And that includes “street dancing” likejookin’and breaking.

“Katie found us when I was about 18 years old and allowed us--me and my friends--to come here and practice for free,” B-boy Nosey said. “And she provided a space for our community. So a big part of my success I definitely owe to Katie and the New Ballet Ensemble.”

Breakdancing actually started in the Bronx decades ago.

“Breaking is one of the four main pillars of hip hop culture, which is this folk culture that we invented here in America in New York City in the 70s,” Nosey said.

Although mostly improvisational acrobatic moves and footwork, breaking consists of four basic moves: top rock, down rock, power moves and freezes.

The Paris Olympics features two events: one for b-boys or men, and another for b-girls or women, all facing off in dance battles.

Dancers earn points for performance, musicality, technique, creativity, variety and personality.

As Nosey described, “This is a complete dance from top to bottom and each dancer is telling a story about who they are and where they’re from and telling their own unique and personal way.”

“B-girl Nana” started her breaking journey 10 years ago in her home country of Japan.

“I kind of started learning from YouTube and other local b-boys back then,” Nana said of how she learned her first moves.

After moving to the states two years ago, she joined Nosey’s crew after he spotted her b-girling on social media.

“He found me through the Instagram because I was actually the only one b-girl, active b-girl in Memphis. I guess I can connect to people through the dancing. Even like with the language, there’s no barriers or anything at all,” exclaimed Nana.

Breakdancing in Memphis has taken a backseat to jookin’ these days. But “B-boy Nosey” and “B-girl Nana” look forward to breaking’s debut in the 2024 Olympics and hope it will help bring more “cypher cats” and “throw downs” back to the city.

Our own Kym “B-Girl KC” Clark took that as a challenge and learned a few “power moves” of her own. If you’d like to see how her “throw down” went, you can watch it below:
